coredata.dc:title: Tribocorrosion behavior of graphene-based solid lubricants biofunctionalized with hyaluronic acid on CoCr surfaces
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: García-Alonso, MC.
Chico, B.
Lozano, RM.
Escudero, ML.
coredata.dc:description: In this work graphene-based solid lubricants biofunctionalized with hyaluronic acid were deposited on CoCr surfaces to decrease wear-corrosion phenomena. Graphene oxide was electrochemically reduced on CoCr in an aqueous suspension of graphene oxide 4 g/L by cyclic voltammetry. Biofunctionalization in phosphate buffer solution with HA 3 g/L (PBS-HA) for 24 h was performed. A pin-on-disk tribometer with an integrated cell was used for tribocorrosion tests. Wear-corrosion tests were performed against AISI316 steel balls applying normal load 1 N, 120 rpm for 500 m, in PBS and PBS-HA. Coefficients of friction in PBS were higher than in PBS-HA. ErGO and HA-ErGO increased wear rate in AISI316 balls, i.e., ErGO hardens the CoCr surface. Biofunctionalization reduces abrasive properties of ErGO deposits.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CoCr alloy
Solid lubricant
Wear-corrosion
Hyaluronic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical reduction of the graphene oxide and functionalization with hyaluronic acid
Coefficient of friction versus distance of modified CoCr surfaces in PBS and PBS -HA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Coefficient of friction (COF) versus distance of CoCr, ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr disk surfaces against AISI316 steel balls under a normal load of 1 N and sliding of 120 rpm in PBS (a) and PBS-HA (b).
Average COF of CoCr, ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr surfaces against AISI316 ball in PBS and PBS-HA.

Secondary electron images of the wear tracks after wear corrosion of CoCr, ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr surfaces after 500 m against AISI316 steel balls under a normal load of 1 N and sliding of 120 rpm in PBS.

a) Secondary electron general view of the different zones of the surface a): outside, interface and wear track and b) detail of stuck deposits (zone 4 in a) in the ErGOCoCr disk after 500 m against AISI316 ball under a normal load of 1 N and sliding of 120 rpm in PBS.
Optical microscope images of the AISI316 ball scar after wear corrosion against CoCr (a) and HA-ErGOCoCr (b) surfaces in PBS-HA.
Wear rate estimated from the mass loss of the AISI316 ball wearing with CoCr, ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr disks after 500 m under a normal load of 1 N and sliding of 120 rpm in PBS and PBS-HA.
Chemical composition of different zones (marked in Fig. 4) in ErGOCoCr disk after wear corrosion in PBS.
Diameter of the AISI316 balls scar, after wear-corrosion tests, against CoCr, ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr disks, in PBS-HA and PBS.
Chemical composition of the stuck deposits on AISI316 ball scars.

head.ce:title: Tribocorrosion behavior of graphene-based solid lubricants biofunctionalized with hyaluronic acid on CoCr surfaces

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro Nacional de Investigaciones Metalúrgicas (CENIM), Consejo Superior de Investigaciones Científicas (CSIC), Avenida Gregorio del Amo, 8, Madrid 28040, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de Investigaciones Biológicas – Margarita Salas (CIB), Consejo Superior de Investigaciones Científicas (CSIC), Calle Ramiro de Maeztu, Madrid, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centro de Investigaciones Biológicas – Margarita Salas (CIB), Consejo Superior de Investigaciones Científicas (CSIC)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centro de Investigaciones Biológicas – Margarita Salas (CIB), Consejo Superior de Investigaciones Científicas (CSIC), Calle Ramiro de Maeztu, Madrid, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work graphene-based solid lubricants biofunctionalized with hyaluronic acid were deposited on CoCr surfaces to decrease wear-corrosion phenomena. Graphene oxide was electrochemically reduced on CoCr in an aqueous suspension of graphene oxide 4 g/L by cyclic voltammetry. Biofunctionalization in phosphate buffer solution with HA 3 g/L (PBS-HA) for 24 h was performed. A pin-on-disk tribometer with an integrated cell was used for tribocorrosion tests. Wear-corrosion tests were performed against AISI316 steel balls applying normal load 1 N, 120 rpm for 500 m, in PBS and PBS-HA. Coefficients of friction in PBS were higher than in PBS-HA. ErGO and HA-ErGO increased wear rate in AISI316 balls, i.e., ErGO hardens the CoCr surface. Biofunctionalization reduces abrasive properties of ErGO deposits.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Although CoCr prostheses possess a high wear-corrosion performance, the continuous sliding in the contact areas in joint replacements destroys the air-formed chromium oxide film as a consequence of wear-corrosion phenomena. The passivating film is quickly removed, releasing metallic ions and particles, and leaving the bare metallic surface exposed to the biological fluids. During the wear process, the loads heavily applied at the microscale result in the atomic bond breakage. The interaction with the physiological medium causes the formation of new atomic bonds. The synergic action of continuous sliding and corrosion generates stuck deposits on the worn joint prosthesis surfaces by means of the called tribochemical wear phenomena. The tribological layers formed are enriched in nanocrystalline graphite and amorphous sp2 carbon, whose fraction of sp2 bonding is about 80% [1]. This is the origin of the formation of the tribochemical layers, whose chemical and mechanical properties work as a barrier against corrosion [2] and wear [3].

Unfortunately, the tribochemical layers do not cover the entire worn surface and metal debris is continuously generated in the areas exposed to wear-corrosion. The debris is composed of metallic ions and sub-micrometric particles of CrOx [4] and, in the worst cases of excessive wear occurring in the edge-loading processes, large CoOx enriched particles. One of the main reasons to replace a prosthesis is because of the adverse biological reactions produced by this metal debris. This hitherto unresolved situation drives the constant search for new protection strategies of the CoCr joint prostheses in order to decrease the wear-corrosion rate.

Several factors of the tribological system can be considered to have influence on the wear rate: substrate surface, lubricant, environment and tribological conditions. Among all of them, the only action possible to reduce the wear rate in joint replacements is the modification of the substrate surface. The approach proposed in this work deals with the formation, stability and large surface coverage with biofuncionalized carbon enriched layers on CoCr surfaces, simulating the tribochemical layers stuck rigidly found on the removed implant surfaces [2,3,5,6–8].

Graphene is defined as a single carbon layer of the graphite structure, whose nature is described by analogy to a polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon of quasi-infinite dimension. Among all the properties of graphene, those which are particularly interesting for our proposal are those related to its outstanding mechanical properties and high chemical inertia.

The strong covalent bond between atoms in the carbon network provides outstanding mechanical properties. In wear-corrosion applications, the graphene monolayer is oriented parallel to the surface, preventing contact between bearing surfaces. Nevertheless, the bond between the carbon sheets is considerably weaker [9] due to Van der Waals forces that easily shear against each other at low friction [10]. In fact, lamellar carbon structure seems to be able to prevent contact between highly loaded surfaces.

Previous research works have established that adequately distributed nanolubricants decrease the sliding frictional losses by supplying a nanolubricant dispersoid which leads to the formation of a steady tribo-film at the sliding surface of a workpiece interface [11]. These special characteristics make graphene considered as a promising solid lubricant that combines interesting properties such as bearing high loads, high thermal stability and versatility in ways of application.

Graphene oxide is an interesting graphene derivate because adds oxygen to the carbon structure such as carboxylic, epoxy and hydroxide groups susceptible to be used as linkers to biomolecules. Carboxylic groups are arranged at the borders of the network structure whereas the epoxy and hydroxide groups are disposed inside the planar structure [12]. The incorporation of these oxygen-functionalized groups confers hydrophilic character, easy dispersion in aqueous solutions and the possibility of functionalization for biomedical applications [13].

However, the incorporation of oxygen in the carbon structure alters the properties of the initial graphene. In order to restore the original mechanical properties of graphene, reduction of the graphene oxide must be carried out [14–17]. A review of the efficiency of the updated reduction methods of graphene oxide makes it quite clear that the complete removal of oxygen-containing functional groups is not achieved [17,18]. For example, our previous XPS results [19] of electrochemically reduced graphene oxide (ErGO) confirmed the presence of carbonyl and epoxy groups in the carbon network after electrochemical reduction. The electrochemical method causes the reduction of carboxyl groups to carbonyl, and potentials such as − 2.1 V reduce carbonyl groups to form CC and leave OH-groups without reduction [20]. As a consequence of reduction of carboxyl groups at the border of the graphene oxide lamellae, high-energy bonds are formed which may react strongly with other edge sites, basal planes or other substances. Carbonyl and epoxy groups in ErGO pave the way for their functionalization, in contrast to graphene which is considered an inert material.

The functionalization of the reduced graphene oxide with biological molecules responsible for the lubrication in joints, such as the hyaluronic acid (HA), can presumably be facilitated in the edges of the carbon network. In optimal conditions, the adsorption of hyaluronic acid onto reduced graphene oxide through the functionalized oxygen-groups is favored, giving rise to a functionalized surface with good biocompatibility [21].

Hyaluronic acid, the main lubricant component of the synovial fluid is composed of a heavy polysaccharide with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. The hyaluronic acid has remarkable viscoelastic properties that contribute decisively to the lubrication of articular cartilage [22]. During movement, the pressure applied to the cartilage results in an increase in the concentration of hyaluronan that is too large to pass through the articular surface, and a decrease in the concentration of proteins that are sufficiently small to pass through. As a consequence, a thick layer is observed at the surface of the articular cartilage which can be attributed to a mixture of proteins, at a low concentration, and high concentration of hyaluronan that acts as a gel-like layer. The strategy of concentrating HA physically adsorbed at the surface of ErGO is intended to mimic the behaviour of the articular cartilage providing lubrication in joints.

So, the main aim of this work is the study of the tribocorrosion behaviour of reduced graphene oxide biofuncionalized with HA on CoCr surfaces. This surface modification is aimed at reducing the wear rate in sliding conditions for different lubricants.

CoCr alloy (ASTM F1537: 27.62% Cr, 5.50% Mo, 0.8% Mn, 0.62% Si, 0.04% C, 0.32% Fe, 0.170% N, 0.10%, Ni, 0.02% W, 0.001% S, 0.003% P and balance Co) was purchased from Edge International as bars of 38 mm diameter. Bars were cut into discs of 3 mm thickness. Discs were mechanically abraded with SiC abrasive paper of decreasing grain size (P340, P600, P1200 and P2000), then polished successively with 3 µm and 1 µm diamond paste. Prior to the surface treatments, all samples were cleaned with distilled water and ethanol.
The commercial pins of AISI316 steel balls of 6 mm diameter were washed with distillated water.

Two lubricant media were used in the wear-corrosion tests: a Phosphate Buffer Saline (PBS) solution with the following chemical composition was used: 0.2 g/L KCl, 0.2 g/L KH2PO4, 8 g/L NaCl, and 1.150 g/L Na2HPO4 anhydrous (Merck); and PBS solution plus 3 g/L HA sodium salt from streptococcus equi (Sigma-Aldrich Chemie GmbH, Schnelldorf, Germany).
The aqueous suspension of GO of 4 g/L was supplied by Grupo Antolin Holding (Burgos, Spain). The corresponding pH (2.3) and conductivity (1.7 mScm−1) values were measured with a Crison pH meter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical reduction of the graphene oxide and functionalization with hyaluronic acid
ce:section.ce:para: Grinding and polished CoCr discs were immersed into 4 g/L graphene oxide aqueous suspension in aerated conditions at room temperature. The authors have observed that CoCr alloys form a native surface oxide film that impairs the reduction of graphene oxide on the CoCr surfaces. The insulating character of the passive film is inefficient for electron transport which has been corroborated with other passive materials, such as Al [23]. This fact meant that the CoCr surfaces had to be polished and, then, immediately immersed in the aqueous suspension of graphene oxide to apply the electrochemical reduction.

Electrochemical experiments were carried out using a three-electrode electrochemical cell. CoCr discs were used as working electrodes, a graphite bar as the auxiliary electrode and Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) electrode was used as reference. An Autolab32 potentiostat/galvanostat was used for the electrochemical experiments. The Electrochemical reduction of Graphene Oxide nanosheets (ErGO) on CoCr surfaces was conducted by using cyclic voltammetry (CV). The potential varied from − 2.1 Vvs. Ag/AgCl to − 0.5 Vvs. Ag/AgCl, at 10 mV/s for 5 cycles. After electrochemical reduction, ErGOCoCr samples were washed in distillated water and air dried. A surface area of 8,04 cm2 was covered with reduced graphene oxide [19].

Functionalization of the ErGOCoCr surfaces with HA was carried out by immersion in an aqueous solution that contained PBS plus 3 g/L HA for 24 h (HA-ErGOCoCr). Then, HA-ErGOCoCr samples were washed in distillated water.

The tribological tests were performed in a pin-on-disc microtest tribometer. Metal on Metal couple was chosen to study the wear-corrosion behaviour of the biofunctionalized CoCr surfaces. Discs of 38 mm diameter and 3 mm thickness were used in the following surface conditions: HA-ErGOCoCr, ErGOCoCr and CoCr surfaces. AISI316 steel balls of 6 mm diameter were used as pins in this configuration.

The wear-corrosion tests were conducted in two different lubricants: PBS and PBS plus 3 g/L HA (PBS-HA), the main lubricant compound in the synovial fluids. The normal load applied was 1 N at 120 rpm for 500 m (11400 cycles). The friction forces and the estimated coefficient of friction (COF) values versus distance were automatically recorded by the computer.

After wear-corrosion tests, both the worn biofuncionalized CoCr surfaces and AISI316 steel balls were characterized by optical microscopy (OM), optical confocal profilometry and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The tracks were observed with an Olympus optical microscope and an optical confocal profilometer Sensofar PLm 2300. SE images were taken with a JEOL-6500 F microscope equipped with a Field Emission Gun (FEG) coupled to an Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrometer.

AISI316 selected as counterpart, which is softer than CoCr, enables the estimation of the wear rate from the loss mass of the AISI316 balls due to the wear-corrosion tests against the different CoCr modified surfaces. The wear rate was estimated from mass loss [24] of the AISI316 steel balls according to the Eq. (1):
(1)
W
s
=
Δ m
F x d x ρ
where W
s is the wear rate of the steel ball (mm3/Nm), Δm is the mass loss (g), F is the applied load (N), d is the sliding distance (m) and ρ is the AISI316 steel density (8,0 ×10−3 g/mm3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coefficient of friction versus distance of modified CoCr surfaces in PBS and PBS -HA
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the COF versus sliding of AISI316 steel balls on CoCr, ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr surfaces subjected to a normal load of 1 N at 120 rpm after 11400 cycles (500 m), under different lubrication conditions in: PBS (a) and PBS-HA(b).

A close observation of COF at the first cycles shows values near 0.1 for ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr surfaces in both lubricant media. However, these low COF values are lost after several cycles at larger sliding distances. This result induces us to think that the reduced graphene oxide deposits could serve as effective solid lubricants at the early stages, but ErGO sheets seem to be weakly adhered to CoCr surfaces and are removed after the first cycles of sliding. The loss of adherence of solid lubricants deposited on metals has been also observed by Regalla et al. [10] in his work about the tribological performance of zinc soft metal coatings in solid lubrication.

Berman et al. [25] reported that a monolayer of graphene on the steel substrate could last for 6.4 × 103 sliding cycles with a low steady friction coefficient (∼0.26) between two sliding steel interfaces by pin-on-disc wear testing. However, the monolayer could not survive for a long period of time in the case of the dry nitrogen environment, and its friction coefficient increased to 0.8 after several hundred cycles, indicating the removal of the coating from the wear track. A possible explanation was that the interaction of the nitrogen with monolayer graphene induced the formation of sp3 carbon which could potentially decrease the wear resistance. Our results seem to follow this trend, i.e., formation of sp3 carbon structure due to the remnant oxygen that increases the COF.

In PBS lubricant medium (Fig. 1a), the tests conducted on the CoCr surfaces show the highest COF values during the sliding. The COF in CoCr surfaces is progressively increasing from 0.45 up to about 0.55 during the first 6800 cycles (300 m). For the remaining 4600 cycles (200 m), the COF remains at a value close to 0.57 until the end of the test. The COF in ErGOCoCr surfaces slightly increases from 0.36 up to about 0.45 during the first 5700 cycles (250 m aprox). Then, the COF remains quite steady at 0.45 until the end of the test. Finally, the COF in HA-ErGOCoCr surfaces slowly increases from 0.39 up to 0.47 until the end of the test. In the case of PBS, the COF is decreased in ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr surfaces compared to CoCr surfaces.

In PBS-HA lubricant medium (Fig. 1b), the tests conducted on the CoCr surfaces show the lowest COF values during the sliding. The COF in CoCr surfaces is progressively increasing from 0.3 up to about 0.45 for the entire duration of the sliding test. The COF in ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr surfaces slightly increases from 0.38 up to about 0.45 during the first 1025 cycles (45 m), and after, the COF remains quite steady at about 0.46 until the end of the test. The COF in both ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr surfaces is practically the same, and the only remarkable difference in the COF is observed in CoCr surfaces. Nevertheless, the COF values remain higher than 0.2, the critical value of self-lubricating materials [26], indicating that ErGO modifications do not behave as a self-lubricant material.

Factors such as the number of ErGO layers, the degree of defects in the reduced graphene oxide structure, the surface on which graphene is physical or chemically bonded, among others, play an important role that determine its wear and adhesion properties [27]. The lubrication properties of ErGO sheets have been ascribed to its intrinsic structure, that is, bonding to the substrate and low resistance to shear owing to lamellar structure [28]. Graphene nanoscroll formed during wear processes of evenly distributed graphene derivate films higher than 500 nm decrease coefficients of friction at low loads and short duration of test [29]. Thickness of the ErGO films achieved in our tests is, approximately, 20–30 nm [19], far away from the optimal thickness. In addition, ErGO is physically adsorbed on CoCr surfaces. The poor adhesion between ErGO and CoCr substrate causes loose bonding that can pucker in the out-of-plane direction. Finally, the incomplete reduction of oxygen-functional groups and the remaining oxygen-groups in the carbon network can act as defects (sp3 bonding) in the graphene structure impairing the mechanical properties of the carbon network. In spite of the low COF values at the few first cycles, this behaviour is not maintained as longer testing time in any condition.

The significant variable affecting to the average COF values is the lubricant medium. The average values of the COF in PBS are higher than those obtained in PBS-HA, verifying the lubricant character of hyaluronic acid. This effect is especially seen on CoCr surfaces decreasing the COF value from 0.49 to 0.27. The continuous sliding under the load of the AISI316 ball against CoCr surfaces immersed in PBS with HA helps to create an intermediate lubricant film between the contact areas thus decreasing and stabilizing the tribocorrosion phenomena.

It has been reported in the literature that the addition of hyaluronan to PBS can decrease the COF of cartilage sliding against cartilage from 0.29 in PBS to 0.12 in PBS with HA [30]. In a pure HA, the carboxyl groups of HA favour the accommodation as an elongated configuration due to the electrostatic repulsion between them that appears strongly interlocked. But changes in the local environment, such as the increase in the salt concentration or ionic composition by the incorporation of PBS, influence the interactions with neighbouring HA molecules and surrounding species changing not only the interaction and conformation of the hyaluronic acid but also the diffusion coefficients of the molecules. Flattening the loosely packed HA chains favours low friction sliding. In this context, the elongated conformation of the HA molecule and the high diffusion coefficient of the solution are beneficial [31] for increasing the lubricant effect.( Fig. 2).

In any case, in the most favourable lubricant condition with hyaluronic acid, ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr surfaces show higher COF than CoCr surfaces, indicating that ErGO modification causes an increase in friction. The increase in friction can be associated with many factors, among the most important ones those related to shear and lamination of the sheets. In our case, the main cause in the increased friction seems to be related to the delamination of the ErGO film from the CoCr surfaces due to the poor adhesion between ErGO and CoCr substrates. This phenomenon has been also observed by other authors [32].

The width and depth of the wear track on the disk surfaces after sliding against the AISI316 steel ball in the lubrication conditions were also measured. The width of the tracks varies from 674 µm in CoCr disks immersed in PBS-HA to close to 800 µm in ErGO disks, independently of the lubricant medium. The depth measured in the tracks varies from 500 µm in the smoother condition, that is CoCr in PBS-HA, to 1800 µm in ErGO disks in PBS. Both width and depth of wear track were the highest for the ErGO disks.

SE images of the worn surfaces of CoCr, ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr disks in PBS appear in Fig. 3. In these images, ErGO biofunctionalized with HA showed smooth worn surfaces. The images show the presence of stuck deposits inside the track, especially in the ErGOCoCr surfaces in PBS.

Chemical composition of several zones in the wear track of the ErGOCoCr surface has been analysed by EDX ( Fig. 4) whose results are shown in Table 1. The analysis of the chemical composition at the different zones revealed, besides the metallic contents coming from the base substrate and PBS, variations in the carbon and oxygen contents and the presence of iron.

The outside wear track area (spectrum 1 in Table 1) contains the typical metallic elements from the CoCr alloy, high oxygen (from passive layer and graphene oxide) and carbon (from graphene oxide) percentage with other components coming from PBS (such as Na and P). In addition, high iron content is accumulated around the track that is an indication of mass transfer from the AISI316 balls. The continuous process of sliding under load promotes the release of metallic ions and particles that entraps inorganic ions from PBS (Na, Cl, P) that are being moved away and accumulated in the outside areas. The small abrasive particles generated during the wear process lead to abrasive wear giving rise to smooth areas (zone 3 in Fig. 4(a)) with a similar chemical composition to the CoCr matrix. In addition, cyclic loading weakens the material that may result in subsurface cracks, caused by surface fatigue, that propagate and lead to material losses as particles.

Finally, stuck deposit areas (zone 4 in Fig. 4(a) and (b)) appear following the sliding direction forming precipitates, mainly composed of carbon and oxygen (comparing spectrum 3 and 4) along with chromium, cobalt and molybdenum from the alloy, and in a lesser proportion, components from PBS (Na and P) and Fe from AISI316 ball. Tribochemical wear involves chemical reactions between the solid surfaces and the surrounding environment. The chemical analysis of stuck deposits on the worn surfaces, confirms that the carbon enriched deposits, compatible with graphitic tribofilms, are formed at rubbing interfaces, which, in a greater uniform coverage on the CoCr surface, could lead to the decrease of wear rates. Liao et al. identified graphitic carbon on explanted worn metal on metal hip joints which suggested the formation of a solid lubricant that should improve wear resistance [33]. This would be the role of the tribochemical layers found in the prostheses retrieval [34]. The tribochemical layer is formed, especially in ErGOCoCr surfaces, that is composed of carbon enriched compounds mixed with the metallic elements accumulated at the bottom of the scratch in the wear track.

The wear rate has been quantified from the profiles and mass losses of the wear scars on the AISI316 ball after wear corrosion against CoCr, ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr disks. Fig. 5 shows, as an example, the optical microscope images of the AISI316 steel ball scar after wear corrosion process against CoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr surfaces in PBS-HA. Broad wear tracks in the direction of sliding appear in the AISI316 balls due to scratching with the disks. These tracks are grooves generated by ploughing, due to the abrasive wear mechanism which is active in all samples (Fig. 4). Black precipitates appeared in AISI316 ball scars after wear corrosion against ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr probably due to mass transfer of graphene compounds to the AISI316 ball (Fig. 5b).

The wear scar diameter has been measured for each lubricant medium. Table 2 shows the diameter of the AISI316 ball scar after wear-corrosion tests against each modified CoCr surface under every lubricant condition.

ErGOCoCr surfaces cause wider scars in both lubricants, PBS and PBS-HA. This result agrees with the depth measured in the tracks of CoCr and ErGOCoCr disks previously described. This can be attributed to the continuous formation of crumpled irregular nanoparticles composed of reduced graphene oxide sheets that act as nano-bearings in the tribocorrosion process. Wear-corrosion tests performed in PBS reveal a higher diameter scar for each pair considered.

Adhering carbon-enriched precipitates appeared in scars after wear corrosion against ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr have been analysed by EDX ( Table 3). The high amounts of carbon are indicative that mass transfer from ErGO deposits to AISI316 steel ball takes place.
This result reveals that retention of ErGO deposits on the CoCr surface is poor, irregular and non-adherent.
The wear rate estimated from the mass loss of the AISI316 balls, after wear corrosion, against CoCr, ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr surfaces appears in Fig. 6.

It can be seen that ErGO surfaces cause the highest wear rate in AISI316L balls in both lubricant media: PBS and PBS-HA. The sliding of the AISI316 balls on the ErGO sheets tend to wrap it creating smaller ErGO nanoscrolls and reducing the surface area covering the substrate. The continuous wear process facilitates the adhesion recombination of ErGO fragments broken as a consequence of both load and sliding, leading to the formation of large areas of wrapped ErGO sheets trapped in the contact area of sliding. Hardness of the particles produced on the tribo-contact zones is high causing high wear on the AISI316 balls. This is similar to that observed by some researchers [35,36]. The ErGO fragments generated due to adhesive wear induce the removal of material and transfer of material from CoCr surfaces to AISI316 ball surface (Fig. 5(b)). The continuous fretting and dragging of these mixed particles of ErGO and CoCr lead to abrasive wear. In this context, it is of great importance to form evenly and firmly distributed reduced graphene oxide layer on the CoCr surfaces.

Meanwhile the further soaking of ErGO deposits for 24 h with hyaluronic acid results in reducing the abrasive properties of ErGO deposits under both lubricant conditions but they are still higher than for CoCr surfaces. Again, the beneficial effect of adding hyaluronic acid to PBS is clearly seen in the wear rate of the AISI316 ball. This behavior agrees with the values of the scar diameter of 316AISI balls against CoCr surfaces that are lower than those obtained for ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr surfaces, independently of the lubricant medium.

In summary, carbon enriched layers have been detected on the 316AISI ball scars revealing mass transfer from ErGO deposits to AISI316 balls. ErGO particles released cause a high wear rate. Nevertheless, the addition of hyaluronic acid on the ErGO induces a slight decrease in the wear rate on AISI316 balls. The special structure, configuration, viscosity, increased wettability and cell adhesion of the hyaluronic acid [37] make it work as a good lubricant even under severe machining conditions, facilitating a continuous lubrication between both contact areas (ball/disc), which enabled the formation of a low-friction tribofilm working. However, the wear rate is always higher in CoCr surfaces with graphene compounds covering the surfaces than in bare CoCr surfaces.

- In the most favourable lubricant condition with hyaluronic acid, ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr surfaces show higher COF than CoCr surfaces, from 0.45 to about 0.3, at initial stages, respectively, indicating that ErGO modification increases the friction with respect to the CoCr surface. This increase in COF is associated with a higher mass loss of AISI316 balls. ErGO surfaces cause the highest wear rate in AISI316L balls in both lubricant media.

- In ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr surfaces small abrasive particles generated during the wear process lead to abrasive wear. In addition, tribochemical deposits are also formed in some areas, especially in ErGOCoCr surfaces, that are composed of carbon enriched precipitates mixed with the metallic elements accumulated at the bottom of the scratch in the wear track.

- Carbon enriched precipitates adhered around AISI316 scars after wear corrosion against ErGOCoCr and HA-ErGOCoCr are indicative that mass transfer from ErGO deposits to AISI316 steel ball exists, and hence the retention of ErGO deposits on the CoCr surface is poor, irregular and non-adherent.

MC Garcia-Alonso: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – original draft; Writing - review & editing. B. Chico: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing. RM Lozano: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. ML Escudero: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Funding: This work was supported by the Spanish Ministry of Science, Innovation and Universities [RTI2018–101506-B-C31, RTI2018–101506-B-C33].

ce:section.ce:para: The authors state that the work “Tribocorrosion behavior of graphene-based solid lubricants biofunctionalized with hyaluronic acid on CoCr surfaces” has not been published previously, that it is not under consideration for publication elsewhere, that its publication is approved by all authors and tacitly by the responsible authorities where the work was carried out, and that, if accepted, it will not be published elsewhere in the same form, in English or in any other language, including electronically without the written consent of the copyright-holder.
